Value-Estimation Analysis

Jiarong Feng

2026-06-03

Overview

This report focuses on value-estimation performance. These are the assessment items where participants estimated either the correlation value or the slope value from a scatterplot.

The add-points tasks are analyzed separately. Here, I keep the correlation and slope estimation analyses separate because they are conceptually different skills and use different prior-knowledge covariates.

The main outcome is absolute error:

abs_error_no_extreme = |participant estimate - correct value|

Lower values mean better performance. Extreme slope entries where |answer| > 10 were preserved in the raw data but excluded from the primary cleaned error variable.

Load scored outcome files

The scoring notebook writes the value-estimation outputs to data/derived/:

  • scored_value_estimation_item_level.csv
  • scored_value_estimation_participant_level.csv
  • participant_outcomes.csv

Those files are created in 02_parse_and_score_outcomes.Rmd.

Data checks

Value-estimation item-level data check
group lecture_type practice_type stat_type n_participants n_item_rows n_valid_rows n_extreme_rows
CC Control Control correlation 20 160 160 0
CP Control PlotWise correlation 20 160 160 0
PC PlotWise Control correlation 20 160 160 0
PP PlotWise PlotWise correlation 20 160 160 0
CC Control Control slope 20 140 134 6
CP Control PlotWise slope 20 140 130 10
PC PlotWise Control slope 20 140 140 0
PP PlotWise PlotWise slope 20 140 138 2

Scoring note. Extreme entries are only expected for slope. The primary error variable excludes those entries, so the slope analysis reflects cleaned value-estimation error rather than a few very large numeric entries.

Descriptive performance

Participant-level mean absolute error

Participant-level value-estimation performance by condition
Statistic type Condition Lecture type Practice type n participants Mean absolute error SD absolute error Median absolute error Mean sign-error rate
Correlation CC Control Control 20 0.336 0.159 0.321 14.4%
Correlation CP Control PlotWise 20 0.316 0.219 0.240 11.2%
Correlation PC PlotWise Control 20 0.333 0.186 0.277 15.0%
Correlation PP PlotWise PlotWise 20 0.290 0.158 0.270 8.1%
Slope CC Control Control 20 0.296 0.350 0.145 7.1%
Slope CP Control PlotWise 20 0.232 0.215 0.157 2.1%
Slope PC PlotWise Control 20 0.363 0.641 0.231 3.6%
Slope PP PlotWise PlotWise 20 0.238 0.129 0.183 2.9%

What stands out descriptively. For correlation estimation, PP has the lowest mean error, while CC is highest. The spread is not huge, so I would not treat this as a strong condition effect by itself.

For slope estimation, the lower-error groups are CP and PP, while PC has the highest error. This looks more patterned than correlation descriptively, but the participant-level variation is still important.

Sign errors

Sign errors by condition
stat_type group n_participants n_scored_trials n_sign_errors sign_error_rate
Correlation CC 20 160 23 14.4%
Correlation CP 20 160 18 11.2%
Correlation PC 20 160 24 15.0%
Correlation PP 20 160 13 8.1%
Slope CC 20 140 10 7.1%
Slope CP 20 140 3 2.1%
Slope PC 20 140 5 3.6%
Slope PP 20 140 4 2.9%

How to read this table. Here n_sign_errors is the count of trials where the participant gave the wrong sign. n_scored_trials is the number of nonzero-answer trials used as the denominator.

Direction mistakes. Correlation sign errors are more common than slope sign errors. For correlation, PP has the lowest sign-error rate ( 8.1% ). For slope, sign errors are low across all conditions.

Correlation value-estimation model

This model uses only the correlation-estimation items.

Model formula

abs_error_no_extreme ~ lecture_type * practice_type + (1 | participantId)
Model-estimated condition means: correlation absolute error
Outcome Condition Estimated mean Lower 95% CI Upper 95% CI
Correlation absolute error CC 0.336 0.255 0.417
Correlation absolute error CP 0.316 0.235 0.397
Correlation absolute error PC 0.333 0.252 0.414
Correlation absolute error PP 0.290 0.209 0.371
Full model results: correlation absolute error
Term Estimate SE Test statistic p-value
Intercept 0.336 0.040 8.465 < .001
PlotWise lecture -0.003 0.056 -0.049 0.961
PlotWise practice -0.021 0.056 -0.365 0.716
PlotWise lecture × PlotWise practice -0.023 0.079 -0.292 0.771

For correlation absolute error, PP has the lowest model-estimated error, while CC has the highest. However, the condition differences are small, and the 95% confidence intervals overlap substantially. The full model also shows no clear lecture effect, practice effect, or lecture × practice interaction. Overall, I would describe this as a weak descriptive trend favoring PP, not evidence of a reliable condition effect for correlation estimation.

Slope value-estimation model

This model uses only the slope-estimation items. Extreme slope entries are excluded through abs_error_no_extreme.

Model formula

abs_error_no_extreme ~ lecture_type * practice_type + (1 | participantId)
Model-estimated condition means: slope absolute error
Outcome Condition Estimated mean Lower 95% CI Upper 95% CI
Slope absolute error CC 0.306 0.131 0.481
Slope absolute error CP 0.237 0.061 0.413
Slope absolute error PC 0.363 0.191 0.535
Slope absolute error PP 0.238 0.066 0.411
Full model results: slope absolute error
Term Estimate SE Test statistic p-value
Intercept 0.306 0.086 3.573 < .001
PlotWise lecture 0.057 0.120 0.475 0.636
PlotWise practice -0.069 0.121 -0.569 0.571
PlotWise lecture × PlotWise practice -0.056 0.170 -0.326 0.745

For slope absolute error, CP and PP have the lowest model-estimated errors, while PC has the highest. However, the confidence intervals overlap substantially across all conditions, and the full model shows no clear lecture effect, practice effect, or lecture × practice interaction. I would interpret this as a descriptive pattern suggesting slightly better slope estimation in the PlotWise practice conditions, but not as evidence of a reliable condition effect.

Condition contrasts

The model coefficients are relative to the reference condition, so I use contrasts to read the condition differences more directly. For value estimation, positive differences mean the first condition has higher error than the second condition. Since lower error is better, positive differences favor the second condition.

Pairwise contrasts

Pairwise comparisons: correlation absolute error
Outcome Contrast Difference Lower 95% CI Upper 95% CI p-value
Correlation Control Control - PlotWise Control 0.003 -0.148 0.154 1
Correlation Control Control - Control PlotWise 0.021 -0.130 0.172 0.984
Correlation Control Control - PlotWise PlotWise 0.046 -0.105 0.197 0.852
Correlation PlotWise Control - Control PlotWise 0.018 -0.133 0.169 0.99
Correlation PlotWise Control - PlotWise PlotWise 0.044 -0.107 0.195 0.873
Correlation Control PlotWise - PlotWise PlotWise 0.026 -0.125 0.177 0.97
Pairwise comparisons: slope absolute error
Outcome Contrast Difference Lower 95% CI Upper 95% CI p-value
Slope Control Control - PlotWise Control -0.057 -0.381 0.266 0.967
Slope Control Control - Control PlotWise 0.069 -0.258 0.396 0.945
Slope Control Control - PlotWise PlotWise 0.068 -0.256 0.391 0.947
Slope PlotWise Control - Control PlotWise 0.126 -0.198 0.450 0.737
Slope PlotWise Control - PlotWise PlotWise 0.125 -0.196 0.446 0.739
Slope Control PlotWise - PlotWise PlotWise -0.002 -0.326 0.323 1

The pairwise contrasts do not show reliable condition differences after Tukey adjustment. For correlation estimation, PP has the lowest estimated error descriptively, but none of the pairwise confidence intervals exclude zero. For slope estimation, PC appears to have higher error than CP and PP, but those differences are also uncertain. Overall, the pairwise results are useful for locating the descriptive pattern, but they do not provide strong evidence that any two conditions differ clearly in value-estimation error.

Modeling with Prior knowledge

This section asks whether baseline definition knowledge helps explain value-estimation performance.

For the correlation-estimation model, I add the coded correlation-definition score. For the slope-estimation model, I add the coded slope-definition score. Scores are standardized before entering the models.

Loaded prior knowledge scores from prior_knowledge_coding_sheet_coded.csv ( long coded sheet ). The coded sheet was converted to participant-level correlation_score and slope_score. The adjusted models below use standardized prior-knowledge scores.

Prior knowledge by condition

Prior knowledge scores by condition
group lecture_type practice_type n n_with_scores mean_correlation_prior sd_correlation_prior mean_slope_prior sd_slope_prior mean_total_prior sd_total_prior
CC Control Control 20 20 0.525 0.255 0.525 0.343 1.05 0.484
CP Control PlotWise 20 20 0.550 0.276 0.550 0.456 1.10 0.661
PC PlotWise Control 20 20 0.525 0.255 0.625 0.425 1.15 0.564
PP PlotWise PlotWise 20 20 0.600 0.205 0.600 0.348 1.20 0.377

Correlation model with prior correlation knowledge

Model formula

abs_error_no_extreme ~ lecture_type * practice_type + correlation_score_z + (1 | participantId)
Prior-knowledge-adjusted condition means: correlation absolute error
Outcome Condition Estimated mean Lower 95% CI Upper 95% CI
Correlation absolute error CC 0.332 0.252 0.411
Correlation absolute error CP 0.316 0.236 0.395
Correlation absolute error PC 0.329 0.250 0.409
Correlation absolute error PP 0.298 0.218 0.378
Full adjusted model results: correlation absolute error
Term Estimate SE Test statistic p-value
Intercept 0.332 0.039 8.575 < .001
PlotWise lecture -0.003 0.055 -0.050 0.96
PlotWise practice -0.016 0.055 -0.299 0.766
Correlation prior knowledge (standardized) -0.041 0.020 -2.093 0.0395
PlotWise lecture × PlotWise practice -0.015 0.077 -0.192 0.848

Slope model with prior slope knowledge

Model formula

abs_error_no_extreme ~ lecture_type * practice_type + slope_score_z + (1 | participantId)
Prior-knowledge-adjusted condition means: slope absolute error
Outcome Condition Estimated mean Lower 95% CI Upper 95% CI
Slope absolute error CC 0.291 0.121 0.460
Slope absolute error CP 0.233 0.063 0.403
Slope absolute error PC 0.375 0.208 0.541
Slope absolute error PP 0.243 0.077 0.410
Full adjusted model results: slope absolute error
Term Estimate SE Test statistic p-value
Intercept 0.293 0.082 3.556 < .001
PlotWise lecture 0.084 0.116 0.723 0.472
PlotWise practice -0.058 0.117 -0.493 0.623
Slope prior knowledge (standardized) -0.106 0.041 -2.566 0.0122
PlotWise lecture × PlotWise practice -0.074 0.164 -0.451 0.653

After controlling for participants’ prior knowledge, the condition patterns for value estimation remain small and uncertain. For correlation estimation, PP still has the lowest adjusted error descriptively, but neither lecture, practice, nor the interaction is significant. Prior correlation knowledge is a significant predictor, with higher prior knowledge associated with lower correlation-estimation error.

For slope estimation, CP and PP have the lowest adjusted errors, while PC is highest descriptively. Again, there is no clear lecture, practice, or interaction effect. Prior slope knowledge is a significant predictor, with higher prior slope knowledge associated with lower slope-estimation error. Overall, these models suggest that baseline knowledge explains more variation in value-estimation performance than the experimental conditions.

Bootstrap uncertainty

The bootstrap resamples participants within each condition. For each bootstrap sample, it recomputes condition mean error and condition differences. This does not create more information; it just gives a nonparametric sense of how stable the observed condition differences are.

Correlation bootstrap

Bootstrap condition means: correlation absolute error
Condition Bootstrap mean error Lower 95% CI Upper 95% CI
CC 0.335 0.268 0.403
CP 0.316 0.235 0.418
PC 0.335 0.259 0.418
PP 0.289 0.224 0.360
Bootstrap condition differences: correlation absolute error
Contrast Mean difference Lower 95% CI Upper 95% CI
CC - CP 0.020 -0.100 0.127
CC - PC 0.001 -0.104 0.102
CC - PP 0.046 -0.049 0.143
CP - PC -0.019 -0.138 0.108
CP - PP 0.026 -0.084 0.145
PC - PP 0.045 -0.060 0.149

Bootstrap read. For correlation estimation, 6 of the 6 bootstrap difference intervals cross zero. That matches the model results: the PP group looks a little lower-error descriptively, but the group differences are not stable enough to treat as clear condition effects.

Slope bootstrap

Bootstrap condition means: slope absolute error
Condition Bootstrap mean error Lower 95% CI Upper 95% CI
CC 0.296 0.163 0.456
CP 0.234 0.147 0.333
PC 0.363 0.167 0.691
PP 0.238 0.184 0.295
Bootstrap condition differences: slope absolute error
Contrast Mean difference Lower 95% CI Upper 95% CI
CC - CP 0.062 -0.107 0.245
CC - PC -0.068 -0.432 0.203
CC - PP 0.058 -0.089 0.228
CP - PC -0.130 -0.471 0.102
CP - PP -0.004 -0.107 0.109
PC - PP 0.126 -0.085 0.466

Bootstrap read. For slope estimation, 6 of the 6 bootstrap difference intervals cross zero. The descriptive pattern points to lower error in the PlotWise-practice groups and higher error in PC, but the bootstrap intervals still leave a lot of uncertainty.

Summary

For correlation estimation, PP is consistently the lowest-error group descriptively, and it also has the lowest sign-error rate. But the differences among conditions are small, the pairwise contrasts are not clear, and the bootstrap intervals for condition differences cross zero. So I would describe this as a descriptive PP advantage, not a strong condition effect.

For slope estimation, the pattern is a little more visible: the PlotWise-practice groups have lower estimated error, while PC has the highest error. Still, the uncertainty is wide. This is worth keeping as a pattern to discuss, but I would avoid claiming that the intervention clearly improved slope estimation.

The most reliable result in this report is actually the prior knowledge adjustment: better coded correlation definitions predict lower correlation-estimation error, and better coded slope definitions predict lower slope-estimation error. That suggests the definition-coding measure is meaningful and is picking up something related to participants’ later estimation performance.

Overall, value estimation does not show a clean condition effect. The condition patterns are directionally interesting.
